seo.title: ORX RANK — Future Readiness Ranking for Universities & Programs
seo.description: ORX RANK measures universities and programs by future readiness, AI-era relevance, and real educational value — beyond prestige.

hero.title: Rankings Built for the Future, Not the Past
hero.subtitle: ORX RANK measures what traditional rankings miss — future readiness, curriculum adaptability, AI integration, and real-world relevance for the next generation of students.

why.label: Why ORX Exists
why.title: Traditional Rankings Look Backward. ORX Looks Forward.
why.p1: Most university rankings reward past reputation, legacy endowments, and academic citations. They measure what institutions were — not what they are becoming. For students making decisions today, that distinction matters more than ever.
why.p2: ORX RANK was created to fill a critical gap: helping students evaluate universities by their readiness for the future — AI integration, curriculum freshness, industry connections, and the real capacity to prepare graduates for a world that changes faster than ever.

futureRelevance.title: Future Relevance
futureRelevance.desc: How well programs align with emerging industry needs and future job markets.

curriculumFreshness.title: Curriculum Freshness
curriculumFreshness.desc: Frequency and depth of curriculum updates to reflect current knowledge.

aiIntegration.title: AI Integration
aiIntegration.desc: Adoption of AI tools, AI-focused courses, and computational thinking.

industryConnection.title: Industry Connection
industryConnection.desc: Strength of partnerships, internships, and employer engagement.

adaptationSpeed.title: Adaptation Speed
adaptationSpeed.desc: How quickly institutions respond to technological and market shifts.

skillTransferability.title: Skill Transferability
skillTransferability.desc: Focus on portable, cross-industry skills over narrow specializations.

transparency.title: Transparency
transparency.desc: Availability of outcome data, graduate tracking, and honest reporting.

studentSignals.title: Verified Student Signals
studentSignals.desc: Real student feedback on learning experience, career preparation, and satisfaction.

country.title: Country Level
country.desc: National education ecosystem, innovation climate, regulatory environment, and student support infrastructure.

university.title: University Level
university.desc: Institutional readiness, research output, industry partnerships, and technological infrastructure.

program.title: Program Level
program.desc: Curriculum design, specialization relevance, graduate outcomes, and teaching methodology.

matters.label: Why This Matters
matters.title: Choosing a University is Choosing Future Capability
matters.description: The difference between a university that prepares you for the next decade and one that taught you about the last one is not just academic — it's career-defining. ORX helps you make that distinction with clarity.

capability.label: of future jobs require skills not yet widely taught

adaptability.label: faster curriculum cycles at top-adapting universities

opportunity.label: of employers prioritize practical AI skills

methodology.label: Methodology
methodology.title: Evidence-First, Transparent, Deterministic
methodology.p1: ORX starts from verified evidence — official sources, structured data, and documented signals — then transforms them into weighted signal scores across three layers: Country, University, and Program.
methodology.p2: The final composite score and confidence are computed deterministically: same evidence always produces the same ranking. No opinions, no prestige bias.

steps.analysis: Signal extraction with trust & freshness weighting

whatIsOrx.a: ORX RANK is a future-readiness ranking system that evaluates universities and programs based on their preparedness for the AI era, curriculum adaptability, industry relevance, and real educational value — not just historical prestige.

isOfficial.q: Is ORX RANK an official ranking?
isOfficial.a: ORX RANK is developed and maintained by our research team. It complements traditional rankings by focusing on dimensions they typically overlook — particularly future readiness and practical relevance.

differentFromQs.q: How is ORX different from QS or THE rankings?
differentFromQs.a: QS and THE primarily measure academic reputation, research output, and citations — largely backward-looking metrics. ORX focuses on forward-looking indicators: AI integration, curriculum freshness, industry partnerships, and graduate preparedness for emerging careers.

ranksOnlyUniversities.a: No. ORX operates at three levels: country, university, and program. This gives students a more complete picture of their educational options.

ranksPrograms.a: Yes. ORX evaluates programs individually for curriculum relevance, specialization strength, and graduate outcomes, providing program-level scores where data is available.

basedOnlyAi.q: Is ORX based only on AI metrics?
basedOnlyAi.a: No. While AI readiness is one signal family, ORX evaluates multiple signal families across three layers — including curriculum freshness, industry connections, skill transferability, transparency, and applied learning — all derived from verified evidence.

howOftenUpdated.a: ORX scores are continuously refined as new data becomes available. Major methodology updates are published annually, with rolling data integration throughout the year.

canImprove.q: Can universities improve their ORX standing?
canImprove.a: Absolutely. Universities can improve by updating curricula, strengthening industry partnerships, integrating AI tools, improving transparency, and engaging with student feedback. ORX is designed to reward genuine improvement.

seo.title: ORX RANK Methodology — How Universities Are Scored & Ranked
seo.description: A transparent deep-dive into how ORX RANK collects evidence, scores signals, computes layers, and ranks universities based on future readiness.

hero.title: How ORX RANK Scores and Ranks Universities
hero.subtitle: ORX doesn't say: "This university is #1 because its name is strong." ORX says: "This university is #1 because its total verified future signals are higher, with higher confidence, after applying deficit and tie-breaking rules."
hero.keyPoint: Rankings are not built on reputation — they are built on 4 logical layers: Evidence → Signal Scores → Layer Scores → Composite Score + Confidence + Ranking Rules.

evidenceBased.title: Evidence-First
evidenceBased.desc: Every score starts from verified evidence — official sources, structured data, and documented signals. No assumptions, no fabricated data.

multiLayer.title: Multi-Layer Architecture
multiLayer.desc: Scoring operates across three independent layers (Country, University, Program) that combine into a single composite score.

forwardLooking.title: Forward-Looking
forwardLooking.desc: ORX weights indicators that predict future success — AI integration, curriculum freshness, industry relevance — over historical prestige.

transparent.title: Full Transparency
transparent.desc: Methodology is published. Confidence levels are visible. Missing data is penalized, never hidden. Limitations are honestly disclosed.

deterministic.title: Deterministic & Reproducible
deterministic.desc: Same inputs always produce the same output. Rankings are computed by formula, not opinion. Re-runs yield identical results.

pipeline.label: Scoring Pipeline
pipeline.title: From Evidence to Final Ranking — 7 Steps
pipeline.subtitle: ORX follows a strict pipeline where each step feeds the next. No shortcuts, no manual overrides.

evidenceCollection.title: Evidence Collection
evidenceCollection.desc: Gather verified evidence from official university sites, government databases, international organizations (OECD, World Bank), and structured data sources.

evidenceScoring.title: Evidence Scoring
evidenceScoring.desc: Each evidence item is scored using trust weight, freshness decay, and extraction confidence to produce a normalized contribution.

signalAggregation.title: Signal Aggregation
signalAggregation.desc: Contributions are grouped by signal family (e.g., AI integration, curriculum velocity), with domain caps to prevent single-source inflation.

layerScoring.title: Layer Scoring
layerScoring.desc: Signal scores combine into three layers: Country (ORX-C), University (ORX-U), and Program (ORX-P), each with weighted sub-signals.

compositeScore.title: Composite Score
compositeScore.desc: Final score computed: ORX = (C × 0.20) + (U × 0.35) + (P × 0.45), with missing-layer penalties applied.

confidenceCalc.title: Confidence Calculation
confidenceCalc.desc: Independent confidence score computed from 6 factors: evidence count, diversity, independence, freshness, completeness, and conflict rate.

ranking.title: Ranking
ranking.desc: Eligible entities ranked by score (descending), then confidence (descending) for tie-breaking. Dense ranking — same score = same rank.

evidence.title: Step 1: Evidence — Where It All Starts
evidence.description: ORX never starts from the university directly. It starts from evidence. Every piece of evidence entering the system carries specific properties that determine its contribution to the final score.

domainCap.title: Domain Cap Rule
domainCap.desc: No single domain can dominate signal scores. ORX caps the contribution of any single source domain to prevent 10 pages from the same website from creating false confidence. This prevents inflation from a single origin.

layers.title: Step 2: Three Scoring Layers
layers.description: After evidence is scored and aggregated into signal families, ORX computes three independent layer scores. Each layer has its own weighted signal families that determine the final layer value (0–100).

country.title: ORX-C — Country Layer
country.subtitle: National ecosystem and AI readiness context

composite.title: Step 3: The Composite Score
composite.description: The final score for any entity is computed from the three layers. The program layer carries the highest weight because ORX believes the program itself is the most direct indicator of student outcomes.

missingLayers.title: Missing Layer Penalties
missingLayers.desc: ORX does not assume completeness. If a layer is missing, the system applies fallback values and confidence penalties — it never awards free points.

confidence.title: Step 4: Score vs. Confidence
confidence.description: Score measures performance and readiness. Confidence measures how much we trust that score. Two universities may have similar scores, but vastly different confidence levels — and the one with higher confidence is ranked higher.
confidence.example: Example: University A scores 87 with confidence 91. University B scores 87 with confidence 70. University A ranks higher because its evidence base is stronger, more diverse, and more recent.

ranking.title: Step 5: How Rankings Are Determined
ranking.description: Only entities that reach 'scored' status enter the ranking. Entities still evaluating or with insufficient data are excluded — they are never ranked with placeholder positions.

eligibility.title: Eligibility
eligibility.desc: Only entities with status = 'scored', sufficient evidence count, minimum independent sources, and core signal families present.

sortOrder.title: Sort Order
sortOrder.desc: Entities are sorted by composite score (highest first). Higher score = higher rank.

tieBreaker.title: Tie-Breaking
tieBreaker.desc: When two entities have the same score, the one with higher confidence ranks higher. This rewards stronger evidence foundations.

denseRank.title: Dense Ranking
denseRank.desc: Same score + same confidence = same rank. No rank gaps. The next distinct score gets the next consecutive rank.

example.title: Worked Example
example.note: University B and C tie on score (87), but B ranks higher due to superior confidence (91 vs 70).

whyDifferent.title: Why a Less Famous University Can Rank Higher
whyDifferent.description: ORX does not measure traditional prestige. It measures documented future readiness. A university that is less globally famous but more adapted, transparent, and AI-ready can legitimately outperform a prestigious institution.
whyDifferent.conclusion: The #1 university in ORX is not "the most famous" — it is "the most proven, the most future-ready, the highest in program relevance, and the most thoroughly evidenced."

improvements.title: What We're Still Improving
improvements.intro: ORX methodology is strong, but we are transparent about areas of active development:

calibration.title: Weight Calibration
calibration.desc: Signal weights and score distributions are being calibrated against broader samples as more universities enter the system.

semanticExtraction.title: Deeper Semantic Extraction
semanticExtraction.desc: Moving from keyword-driven heuristics to deeper semantic reasoning for evidence extraction and signal classification.

disciplineSpecific.title: Discipline-Specific Scoring
disciplineSpecific.desc: Some disciplines require deeper rubrics than others. We are developing specialized scoring frameworks for high-demand fields.

whatIsOrx.title: What is ORX RANK and Why It Matters
whatIsOrx.excerpt: An introduction to the future-readiness ranking system that evaluates universities beyond traditional prestige metrics.

sections.heading: ORX Doesn't Say "This University is #1 Because of Its Name"
sections.body:
ORX RANK does not measure prestige, reputation, or brand recognition. Instead, it says: "This university ranks first because the total of its documented future-readiness signals is higher than others, with higher confidence, and after applying penalty and tie-breaking rules."

This means that a less globally famous university could outrank a prestigious one — if it has fresher curricula, better AI integration, stronger industry links, and more transparent, well-documented evidence.

sections.heading: The Four Logical Layers Behind Every Score
sections.body:
ORX does not start from the university itself. It starts from evidence — real, documented data points collected from official sources. Each piece of evidence carries properties like source type, trust level, freshness date, and extraction confidence.

These evidence items are then transformed through four layers:

1. Evidence → Raw data collected and validated
2. Signal Scores → Evidence grouped into signal families (e.g., AI integration, curriculum freshness)
3. Layer Scores → Signal families weighted into Country (ORX-C), University (ORX-U), and Program (ORX-P) scores
4. Composite Score → Final weighted combination + confidence calculation + ranking rules

sections.heading: How Evidence Becomes Points
sections.body:
Each evidence item contributes points using a clear formula:

Contribution = Trust Weight × Decay Multiplier × Extraction Confidence

• Trust Weight: High trust = 1.0, Medium = 0.7, Low = 0.4
• Decay Multiplier: 0–6 months = 1.0, 6–12 = 0.9, 12–18 = 0.75, 18–24 = 0.6, 24–36 = 0.4, 36+ = 0
• Extraction Confidence: Normalized from 0 to 1

A critical rule prevents any single domain from dominating: the Domain Cap limits how much one source can contribute, preventing artificial inflation from repetitive sources.

sections.heading: The Three Evaluation Layers
sections.body:
ORX evaluates every entity across three distinct layers, each with its own signal families:

ORX-C (Country – 20% of final score): Measures the national environment — AI ecosystem, government readiness, digital infrastructure, talent environment, and policy maturity.

ORX-U (University – 35% of final score): Measures institutional readiness — curriculum update velocity, AI integration, applied learning, flexible learning, transparency, student signal, and research compute.

ORX-P (Program – 45% of final score): Measures program-level preparedness — future skill alignment, curriculum freshness, AI workflow exposure, transferability, applied industry signal, and student value signal.

The program layer carries the most weight because ORX believes what you actually study matters more than where you study it.

sections.heading: The Composite Score and Confidence
sections.body:
The final ORX score is calculated as:

ORX = (Country × 0.20) + (University × 0.35) + (Program × 0.45)

But the score alone isn't enough. ORX also calculates a Confidence rating based on six factors: evidence count, evidence diversity, source independence, evidence freshness, signal completeness, and conflict rate.

Two universities may have similar scores, but vastly different confidence levels. The one with higher confidence is ranked higher because the evidence behind its score is more reliable.

sections.heading: How Rankings Are Determined
sections.body:
Only entities that meet strict eligibility thresholds enter the ranking: minimum confidence ≥ 40, minimum evidence count, minimum independent sources, and core signal families present.

Entities that don't meet these thresholds remain in "evaluating" or "insufficient" status — they don't receive a rank.

For eligible entities, ranking follows deterministic rules:
1. Sort by score (highest first)
2. Break ties by confidence (highest first)
3. Dense ranking — same score and confidence = same rank

If any layer is missing, ORX applies a fallback value with a 20% confidence penalty per missing layer. Missing data never gives a free pass — it always hurts.

futureReadiness.title: Why Future Readiness Should Guide Your University Choice
futureReadiness.excerpt: How the gap between traditional rankings and real-world outcomes is reshaping how students should evaluate institutions.

sections.heading: The Gap Between Reputation and Readiness
sections.body:
For decades, students chose universities based on name recognition, historical prestige, and legacy rankings. But the world has changed faster than most institutions have adapted.

A university that was excellent in 2010 may still carry its reputation in 2026 — but its curricula, teaching methods, and industry connections may not have kept pace with the AI revolution, the shift to remote work, or the emergence of entirely new career fields.

Future readiness measures what matters now: Is this institution preparing you for the world you'll actually graduate into?

sections.heading: What ORX Measures That Others Don't
sections.body:
Traditional rankings rely heavily on research output, peer surveys, and employer reputation — all backward-looking metrics. ORX RANK focuses on forward-looking signals:

• Curriculum Update Velocity — How quickly does the institution modernize its programs?
• AI Integration — Are AI tools and concepts embedded in the learning experience?
• Applied Learning — Does the program include real-world projects, industry placements, or lab work?
• Transferability — Will the skills you learn work across industries and borders?

These signals are measured with real evidence, not surveys or opinions.

sections.heading: Why the Program Matters More Than the University
sections.body:
ORX assigns 45% of the total weight to the program layer — more than the university (35%) or country (20%). This reflects a fundamental insight: your specific program of study has the greatest impact on your career preparedness.

A mediocre program at a famous university may leave you less prepared than an excellent program at a lesser-known institution. ORX captures this distinction because it evaluates programs independently, with their own signal families and evidence base.

sections.heading: The Country Context Matters Too
sections.body:
Where you study shapes your experience beyond the classroom. ORX's Country layer (ORX-C) evaluates five dimensions of the national education ecosystem:

• AI Ecosystem strength (25%)
• Government AI readiness (20%)
• Digital infrastructure (20%)
• Talent and skills environment (20%)
• Policy maturity (15%)

A strong program in a country with weak digital infrastructure or limited AI policy will score lower than the same program in a more supportive ecosystem. Context matters.

sections.heading: Making Better Decisions with Evidence
sections.body:
The most important takeaway for students is this: don't rely on a university's name alone. Look at the evidence.

ORX provides transparent, documented signals that show exactly why a university or program scored the way it did. Every score is backed by real evidence — official websites, government data, international databases — not opinions or surveys.

When you choose based on evidence of future readiness, you're not betting on the past. You're investing in your future.

bestCountriesAi.title: Best Countries for AI-Era Education
bestCountriesAi.excerpt: A country-level analysis of which education ecosystems are best positioned for the artificial intelligence revolution.

sections.heading: Why Country-Level Analysis Matters
sections.body:
A university doesn't exist in a vacuum. The national environment — government policies, digital infrastructure, industry presence, and talent ecosystems — profoundly shapes the quality of education you receive.

ORX's Country layer (ORX-C) evaluates each country across five signal families, accounting for 20% of the final composite score. A strong country context can elevate good programs, while a weak one can hold back even excellent institutions.

sections.heading: The Five Signals of National Readiness
sections.body:
ORX-C evaluates countries using these weighted signal families:

• AI Ecosystem (25%) — Presence of AI companies, research labs, startups, and venture capital in AI.
• Government AI Readiness (20%) — National AI strategies, dedicated agencies, and public investment in AI research.
• Digital Infrastructure (20%) — Internet speed, connectivity, cloud computing access, and data center presence.
• Talent & Skills Environment (20%) — STEM graduation rates, coding literacy, international talent attraction.
• Policy Maturity (15%) — Data protection laws, AI ethics frameworks, and regulatory clarity.

Each signal is scored from 0 to 100 based on documented evidence from international organizations, government publications, and verified databases.

sections.heading: What Makes a Country Score High
sections.body:
Countries that score highest on ORX-C typically share several characteristics:

1. Active national AI strategy with measurable investment
2. Strong digital infrastructure supporting remote and hybrid learning
3. High density of technology companies offering internships and research partnerships
4. Progressive policies on data, AI ethics, and student mobility
5. Track record of producing STEM talent that is globally competitive

Importantly, ORX doesn't favor large economies automatically. A smaller country with focused AI policy and excellent digital infrastructure can outscore a larger economy with fragmented efforts.

sections.heading: How Country Scores Affect Your University Ranking
sections.body:
The country score contributes 20% to the final ORX composite. This means:

• A university in a country scoring 90 on ORX-C gets a stronger baseline than one in a country scoring 60
• But it's not deterministic — a strong university (35%) and strong program (45%) can compensate for a weaker country context
• If the country layer is missing or insufficient, ORX applies a fallback score of 50 with a 20% confidence penalty

This design ensures that country context matters but doesn't dominate. A great program in an emerging AI country still has a path to a high ORX score.

sections.heading: Choosing Your Study Destination Wisely
sections.body:
When selecting a country for your studies, consider these ORX-informed questions:

• Does this country have a clear AI strategy and digital infrastructure?
• Are there tech companies and research institutions nearby for internships?
• What is the regulatory environment for data science and AI?
• Does the country attract and retain international talent?

ORX doesn't tell you which country is "best" in absolute terms — it tells you which countries provide the strongest ecosystem for future-ready education, backed by documented evidence rather than assumptions.

universitiesAdapting.title: Universities Adapting Fastest to Future Jobs
universitiesAdapting.excerpt: Which institutions are leading the transformation in curriculum design, industry partnerships, and technology integration.

sections.heading: Adaptation Is Not Optional
sections.body:
The pace of technological change means that universities standing still are falling behind. In the AI era, curriculum relevance has a shorter shelf life than ever. ORX measures institutional adaptation through seven signal families in the University layer (ORX-U), which contributes 35% to the final score.

Universities that adapt fastest are those with documented evidence of curriculum updates, AI tool integration, and industry-responsive program design.

sections.heading: The Seven University Signals
sections.body:
ORX-U evaluates universities using these weighted signals:

• Curriculum Update Velocity (20%) — How frequently does the institution revise its programs?
• AI Integration (20%) — Are AI tools, courses, and research embedded across faculties?
• Applied Learning (18%) — Lab work, capstone projects, industry placements, and real-world problem-solving.
• Flexible Learning (12%) — Online options, modular programs, micro-credentials, and non-traditional pathways.
• Transparency & Data Freshness (10%) — Does the university publish clear, up-to-date information?
• Student Signal (10%, capped) — Student reviews and satisfaction, with caps to prevent manipulation.
• Research & Compute (10%) — Access to computational resources, research labs, and data infrastructure.

Each signal is scored from evidence — official pages, course catalogs, government databases — never from surveys or paid placements.

sections.heading: Why Faster Adapters Score Higher
sections.body:
ORX's Decay Model means that older evidence loses value over time. A university that modernized its curriculum 3 years ago gets less credit than one that did it 6 months ago.

The decay schedule:
• 0–6 months: Full value (1.0)
• 6–12 months: 0.9
• 12–18 months: 0.75
• 18–24 months: 0.6
• 24–36 months: 0.4
• 36+ months: 0 (no contribution)

This means a university must continuously produce fresh evidence of adaptation. Past achievements don't sustain a high score — only ongoing improvement does.

sections.heading: The Domain Cap: Preventing Artificial Inflation
sections.body:
ORX applies a Domain Cap rule that limits how much evidence from a single source domain can contribute to a signal family. This prevents a university from inflating its score by publishing many pages on the same topic from the same website.

For example, 10 pages from a university's own website about AI integration won't count as 10 independent evidence items. The cap ensures that score increases require genuine breadth of evidence, not marketing volume.

This is one of the key mechanisms that makes ORX resistant to gaming.

sections.heading: What This Means for Students
sections.body:
When evaluating universities, don't just look at their current reputation. Ask:

• When was the last time the curriculum was updated?
• Are AI tools integrated into teaching across departments?
• Does the university offer applied learning opportunities with industry partners?
• Is data about programs transparent and recent?

ORX surfaces these signals automatically from documented evidence. A university that ranks high on ORX-U is one that is actively preparing for the future — not coasting on past achievements.

strongestPrograms.title: Strongest Programs for the Next Decade
strongestPrograms.excerpt: Program-level analysis of specializations with the highest projected demand and best preparation for emerging careers.

sections.heading: Why ORX Gives Programs the Highest Weight
sections.body:
In the ORX composite formula, the Program layer (ORX-P) carries 45% of the total weight — more than the University (35%) or Country (20%). This is a deliberate design choice.

Your specific program determines what you actually learn, what skills you develop, and how employable you are. A strong program at a lesser-known university can prepare you better than a weak program at a famous one. ORX captures this reality.

sections.heading: The Six Program Signal Families
sections.body:
ORX-P evaluates programs using six weighted signal families:

• Future Skill Alignment (25%) — Does the curriculum teach skills projected to be in demand? Are graduates ready for AI-augmented work?
• Curriculum Freshness (20%) — When was the program last updated? Does it reflect current industry practices?
• AI Workflow Exposure (18%) — Are students exposed to AI tools, automation workflows, and data-driven decision making?
• Transferability (15%) — Can the skills learned transfer across industries, roles, and countries?
• Applied Industry Signal (12%) — Industry partnerships, internship programs, capstone projects with real companies.
• Student Value Signal (10%, capped) — Student reviews and outcomes, with a cap to prevent score manipulation.

The cap on student signal is important: ORX does not allow subjective reviews to dominate what should be an evidence-based assessment.

sections.heading: How Programs Are Scored
sections.body:
Each signal family within ORX-P is scored from 0 to 100 using documented evidence. The contributions from individual evidence items follow the same formula:

Contribution = Trust Weight × Decay Multiplier × Extraction Confidence

Evidence is aggregated within each signal family, subject to the domain cap, and then normalized. The final ORX-P score is the weighted sum of all six signal scores.

This means a program must have strong, diverse, fresh evidence across multiple signal families to score well. One strong area cannot compensate for neglect in others.

sections.heading: Programs That Are Leading
sections.body:
Programs scoring highest on ORX-P typically share these characteristics:

1. Regular curriculum updates reflecting industry trends and emerging technologies
2. Embedded AI tools and data literacy across courses, not just in computer science
3. Strong industry partnerships that produce real internship and employment pipelines
4. Focus on transferable skills — critical thinking, systems design, cross-disciplinary problem solving
5. Transparent outcomes data showing what graduates actually do

Notably, some of the strongest programs may not be at the most famous universities. ORX reveals programs that deliver real value regardless of institutional prestige.

sections.heading: What to Look For When Choosing a Program
sections.body:
Use these ORX-informed criteria when evaluating programs:

• How recently was the curriculum last revised? (Freshness matters)
• Does the program include AI-related coursework, even if it's not a CS program?
• Are there documented industry partnerships or applied projects?
• Can you find transparent data about graduate outcomes?
• Does the program teach skills that work across multiple industries?

ORX scores programs on exactly these dimensions, using documented evidence rather than marketing claims. The goal is to help you find programs that will be relevant not just today, but throughout the next decade.

aiReshaping.title: How AI is Reshaping Higher Education
aiReshaping.excerpt: The fundamental ways artificial intelligence is changing how universities teach, research, and prepare students.

sections.heading: The AI Transformation Is Already Here
sections.body:
Artificial intelligence is not a future concern for higher education — it's a present reality. From automated grading to AI-assisted research, from personalized learning paths to AI-generated course content, the technology is already reshaping how universities operate.

ORX tracks this transformation through its AI Integration signal family (weighted 20% in ORX-U) and AI Workflow Exposure signal (weighted 18% in ORX-P). Universities and programs that embrace AI score higher because they're preparing students for the world as it is, not as it was.

sections.heading: How ORX Measures AI Readiness
sections.body:
ORX doesn't just check whether a university mentions AI on its website. It evaluates documented evidence of genuine AI integration:

• Are AI tools used in teaching across departments?
• Does the institution offer AI-specific courses beyond computer science?
• Is there evidence of AI research infrastructure — compute resources, data labs, partnerships?
• Do programs include hands-on experience with AI workflows and automation?

Each evidence item is weighted by trust level, freshness, and extraction confidence. Recent, high-quality evidence from independent sources scores highest.

sections.heading: The Three Levels of AI Adoption
sections.body:
ORX observes three distinct levels of AI adoption in higher education:

Level 1 — Awareness: The institution acknowledges AI but hasn't integrated it meaningfully. Course catalogs may mention AI but without embedded tools or updated curricula.

Level 2 — Integration: AI tools are used in teaching and research. Some programs include AI-related coursework. The institution has some AI infrastructure.

Level 3 — Transformation: AI is embedded across the institution — in curriculum design, research methodology, student services, and administrative processes. Programs are designed around AI-era competencies.

Universities at Level 3 score highest on ORX because they show the most comprehensive, documented evidence of AI readiness.

sections.heading: Why This Matters for Your Career
sections.body:
Regardless of your field of study — medicine, law, business, engineering, design — AI will reshape your profession. Students who graduate from programs with strong AI exposure will have a significant advantage.

ORX's AI Workflow Exposure signal (ORX-P) specifically measures whether programs prepare students to work with AI tools, understand automation, and apply data-driven decision making. This isn't limited to computer science — it applies to every discipline.

A law student who has worked with AI-powered legal research tools is more prepared than one who hasn't. A business student trained in AI-driven analytics has an edge. ORX captures these distinctions.

sections.heading: The Future of AI in Education
sections.body:
The AI transformation in higher education will accelerate. ORX is designed to track this evolution continuously, with evidence decay ensuring that scores reflect current reality, not past achievements.

As AI tools become more sophisticated, ORX's methodology will evolve too. But the core principle remains: measure real, documented evidence of how well institutions prepare students for an AI-transformed world. No assumptions, no reputation bias, no guesswork — just evidence.

traditionalMiss.title: Why Traditional Rankings Miss Critical Signals
traditionalMiss.excerpt: An honest examination of what legacy ranking methodologies fail to capture — and why it matters for students.

sections.heading: The Problem with Backward-Looking Metrics
sections.body:
Traditional university rankings — QS, THE, ARWU — rely heavily on metrics that look backward: historical research output, peer reputation surveys, employer perception polls, and faculty-to-student ratios. These metrics tell you what an institution was, not what it is becoming.

ORX was built to address this gap. While traditional rankings measure past achievement and accumulated prestige, ORX measures current readiness and future preparedness using fresh, documented evidence.

sections.heading: What Traditional Rankings Measure
sections.body:
Most traditional ranking systems weight their scores toward:

• Academic Reputation (often 30–40%) — Based on peer surveys, which naturally favor well-known institutions
• Research Output — Publication counts and citations, which reflect historical research investment
• Employer Reputation — Surveys of large employers, biased toward established brand names
• Faculty Ratios — Staff-to-student numbers, which don't capture teaching quality or curriculum relevance

None of these metrics directly measure whether a university is preparing students for future careers, integrating AI, updating curricula, or building industry partnerships.

sections.heading: What They Miss: The ORX Perspective
sections.body:
ORX identifies several critical signals that traditional rankings consistently miss:

1. Curriculum Freshness — How recently programs were updated. Traditional rankings don't measure this at all.
2. AI Integration — Whether AI tools and concepts are embedded in teaching. Not captured by traditional metrics.
3. Applied Learning — Real-world projects, industry placements, and hands-on experience. Rarely measured.
4. Transparency — Whether the institution publishes clear, current data. Not a factor in traditional rankings.
5. Program-Level Quality — Traditional rankings operate at the institution level; ORX evaluates individual programs.
6. Evidence Freshness — Traditional surveys are conducted annually or less. ORX uses continuous evidence with time decay.

sections.heading: The Reputation Feedback Loop
sections.body:
Traditional rankings create a self-reinforcing cycle: prestigious universities attract the best students and most funding, which produces more research, which improves their ranking, which attracts more prestige.

This feedback loop makes it nearly impossible for newer or less well-known institutions to break through — even if they offer better, more relevant education for today's job market.

ORX breaks this loop by scoring from evidence, not reputation. A university that no one has heard of but that has fresh curricula, strong AI integration, and excellent industry partnerships can score higher than a famous university resting on its historical achievements.

sections.heading: Making Informed Decisions
sections.body:
This doesn't mean traditional rankings are worthless — they provide useful data points about research capacity and institutional size. But they should not be your only guide.

ORX complements traditional rankings by adding the dimensions they miss: future readiness, curriculum relevance, AI preparation, and evidence-based transparency.

As a student, the smartest approach is to consider multiple perspectives. Traditional rankings tell you about an institution's past. ORX tells you about its preparedness for your future. Together, they provide a more complete picture for one of the most important decisions of your life.

intro.title: Introduction
intro.content: At Connect Study World, we are committed to protecting your privacy and personal data. This policy explains how we collect, use, share, and protect your information when using our platform. By using our services, you agree to the practices described in this policy.
intro.scope: This policy applies to all users of the Connect Study World platform, including students, educational institutions, and partners worldwide.

dataCollected.title: Data We Collect
dataCollected.subtitle: We collect the following categories of personal information:
items: Full name, email address, and phone number
Academic information (GPA, certificates, desired major)
Search preferences and selected universities
Session and browsing data to improve user experience
Passport and identity documents (when submitting applications)
Financial information (budget preferences, not payment details)
Communication preferences and language settings
Device information, IP address, and browser type

howWeUse.title: How We Use Your Data
howWeUse.content: We use your personal data to provide and improve our services. Specifically:
items: Process and manage your university applications
Match you with suitable programs based on your profile
Communicate important updates about your applications
Personalize your browsing experience and recommendations
Analyze platform usage to improve our services
Comply with legal obligations and prevent fraud

dataSharing.title: Data Sharing with Educational Institutions
dataSharing.content: When you submit an application to a university or educational institution through our platform, we share your personal data (name, email, phone, academic information) with that institution for the purpose of processing your admission application and contacting you. We will not share your data with any institution you have not explicitly consented to.
dataSharing.aiNote: AI may be used to filter and rank applications before sending them to the institution, but this does not involve sharing additional data.
dataSharing.thirdPartyNote: We do not sell your personal data to third parties for marketing purposes.

cookies.title: Cookies & Tracking Technologies
cookies.content: We use cookies and similar tracking technologies to enhance your experience on our platform.
items: Essential cookies: Required for basic platform functionality (login, session management)
Analytics cookies: Help us understand how users interact with our platform
Preference cookies: Remember your language, currency, and display settings
Performance cookies: Monitor platform speed and optimize loading times
cookies.optOut: You can manage cookie preferences through your browser settings. Disabling essential cookies may affect platform functionality.

thirdParty.title: Third-Party Services
thirdParty.content: We integrate with trusted third-party services to provide our platform functionality:
items: Cloud hosting and infrastructure providers
Email and communication services
Payment processing partners (for application fees)
Analytics and monitoring tools
thirdParty.note: All third-party providers are carefully vetted and bound by data protection agreements.

internationalTransfers.title: International Data Transfers
internationalTransfers.content: As a global platform serving students and institutions across 215+ countries, your data may be transferred and processed in countries other than your country of residence. We ensure adequate safeguards are in place for all international transfers, including standard contractual clauses and compliance with applicable data protection regulations.

retention.title: Data Retention
retention.content: We retain your personal data for as long as your account is active or as needed to provide services. Incomplete application data is deleted after 12 months of inactivity.
details: Active account data: Retained while your account is active
Application data: Retained for 3 years after last activity for reference
Incomplete applications: Deleted after 12 months of inactivity

rights.title: Your Rights
rights.subtitle: Under applicable data protection laws, you have the following rights:
items: Right to access your personal data and know how it's used
Right to correct any inaccurate data
Right to delete your personal data (right to be forgotten)
Right to withdraw consent for data sharing at any time
Right to request a copy of your data in a readable format
Right to restrict processing of your personal data
Right to object to automated decision-making
Right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority

deleteRequest.title: Data Deletion Request
deleteRequest.content: You can request deletion of all your personal data from our platform by contacting us via email. We will process your request within 30 business days.
deleteRequest.note: Please note that some data may be retained for legal compliance purposes even after a deletion request.

security.title: Data Security
security.content: We implement comprehensive security measures to protect your data:
items: SSL/TLS encryption for all data in transit
AES-256 encryption for stored sensitive documents
Regular security audits and vulnerability assessments
Role-based access controls limiting data access to authorized personnel
Multi-factor authentication for administrative access
24/7 security monitoring and incident response

minors.title: Children & Minors
minors.content: Our platform is designed for users aged 16 and above. We do not knowingly collect personal data from children under 16. If you believe a minor has provided us with personal data, please contact us immediately and we will take steps to delete that information.

partnerDPA.title: Partner Data Processing Agreement
partnerDPA.content: All partner educational institutions are bound by a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) that governs how they use student data shared through our platform. Key provisions include:
items: Data may only be used for processing admission applications
Partners must implement adequate security measures
Partners must delete student data upon request
Partners may not share data with unauthorized third parties
Annual compliance audits are conducted

updates.title: Policy Updates
updates.content: We may update this privacy policy from time to time to reflect changes in our practices, technology, or legal requirements. We will notify you of significant changes via email or a prominent notice on our platform. Your continued use of our services after updates constitutes acceptance of the revised policy.

legal.title: Legal Basis for Processing
legal.content: We process your personal data based on the following legal grounds:
items: Consent: When you explicitly agree to data processing (e.g., submitting an application)
Contract performance: To fulfill our service obligations to you
Legitimate interest: To improve our services and ensure platform security
Legal obligation: To comply with applicable laws and regulations

contact.title: Contact Us
contact.content: For any privacy or data protection inquiries, contact us at:
contact.responseTime: We aim to respond to all privacy inquiries within 48 hours.

teacherPrivacy.title: Teacher Privacy Policy & Data Protection
teacherPrivacy.subtitle: How we collect, use, and protect your data as a teacher on our platform

intro.title: Introduction
intro.body: This policy explains how we collect, use, and protect your personal data as a registered teacher on Connect Study World. We are committed to safeguarding your privacy and ensuring the security of your information in accordance with applicable laws and regulations.

personalData.title: Personal Data We Collect
personalData.body: We collect the following data when you register as a teacher:

sessionData.title: Teaching Session Data
sessionData.body: We record the following data related to your teaching sessions:

evaluationData.title: Evaluation Data
evaluationData.body: We retain the evaluations you provide for students, including:

documentsData.title: Uploaded Documents
documentsData.body: We retain documents you upload for verification and documentation purposes, including identity documents, academic certificates, and experience certificates. These are stored securely with encryption and are only accessible by authorized administrators.

howWeUse.title: How We Use Your Data
howWeUse.body: We use your data for the following purposes:

items.0: Verifying your identity and qualifications as a teacher
items.1: Managing and scheduling your teaching sessions
items.2: Tracking the progress of students assigned to you
items.3: Processing your payments and financial dues
items.4: Improving the quality of our educational services

dataSharing.title: Data Sharing
dataSharing.body: We do not sell your personal data to any third party. We may share basic information (such as your name and specialization) with students assigned to you to facilitate the educational process. We will not share your personal documents or financial information with any external party without your explicit consent.

security.title: Data Security
security.body: We implement strict security measures to protect your data:

items.0: Data encryption in transit and at rest

rights.title: Your Rights as a Teacher
rights.body: You have the following rights regarding your data:

items.0: Access all your stored personal data
items.1: Request correction of any inaccurate data
items.2: Request deletion of your data (subject to legal obligations)
items.3: Object to processing your data for certain purposes
items.4: Request a copy of your data in a portable format
items.5: Withdraw your consent at any time

deleteRequest.title: Data Deletion Request
deleteRequest.body: You can request deletion of your account and all associated data by contacting our privacy team. We will process your request within 30 days. Please note that some data may be retained for a limited period in accordance with legal requirements.

contact.title: Contact Us
contact.body: For any inquiries about your data privacy as a teacher, you can contact our data protection team:

alphabet-map.title: The Complete Russian Alphabet
alphabet-map.objective: Learn all 33 Cyrillic letters: 10 vowels, 21 consonants, and 2 signs — with pronunciation for each.
canDoOutcomes: Can name and pronounce all 33 Russian letters.
Can identify false friends.
Can read simple syllables.
teacherNotes: Start with the full alphabet overview.

sound-rules.title: Alphabet II
sound-rules.objective: Expand decoding to consonant contrasts and soft/hard cues.
sound-rules.content: Sound rules is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can decode new consonant pairs in simple words.
teacherNotes: Use correction chain: stop, isolate, blend.

handwriting-decoding.title: Stress and Pronunciation
handwriting-decoding.objective: Notice stress and reduce unstressed vowels in simple words.
handwriting-decoding.content: Handwriting decoding is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can mark and pronounce stress in high-frequency words.

greetings.title: Greetings
greetings.objective: Use common Russian greetings in formal and informal classroom contexts.
greetings.content: Greetings is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can open a short classroom greeting exchange.
teacherNotes: Use board dictation with slow pace.

self-introduction.title: Self-Introduction
self-introduction.objective: Introduce yourself with name, origin, and role in class.
self-introduction.content: Self introduction is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can introduce self in one short statement.

personal-information.title: Personal Information
personal-information.objective: State key personal information: name, age, country, and study focus.
personal-information.content: Personal information is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can share name, age, and origin in class.
teacherNotes: Force turn-taking with strict time box.

numbers-dates-time.title: Numbers, Age, Dates, Time
numbers-dates-time.objective: State age, date, and time in common classroom contexts.
numbers-dates-time.content: Numbers, dates, and time is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can state age, date, and class time.
teacherNotes: Use board clocks and date cards.
Keep drills oral first then written.

directions-places.title: Directions and Places
directions-places.objective: Ask and answer for location and directions inside campus settings.
directions-places.content: Directions and places is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can describe location of classroom objects.

shopping-transport.title: Shopping and Transport
shopping-transport.objective: Use practical Russian for buying essentials and public transport routines.
shopping-transport.content: Shopping and transport is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can respond to simple teacher commands.

university-vocabulary.title: University Vocabulary
university-vocabulary.objective: Build essential vocabulary for classes, schedules, and campus communication.
university-vocabulary.content: University vocabulary is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can follow two-step class instructions.

classroom-phrases.title: Pronouns and Basic Identity
classroom-phrases.objective: Use basic pronouns and identity sentences in classroom introductions.
classroom-phrases.content: Classroom phrases is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can introduce self with pronouns.
Can identify another person using он/она.

instructions-questions.title: Gender and Singular/Plural
instructions-questions.objective: Recognize gender and singular/plural forms for basic nouns.
instructions-questions.content: Instructions and questions is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can identify masculine/feminine noun endings.
Can switch simple nouns from singular to plural.

reading-notices.title: Basic Study Verbs
reading-notices.objective: Use high-frequency study verbs in present tense classroom contexts.
reading-notices.content: Reading notices is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can say what they read/write/listen to in class.
teacherNotes: Prioritize chunk practice over full conjugation tables.

forms-labels.title: Question Patterns
forms-labels.objective: Use basic question structures for classroom communication.
forms-labels.content: Forms and labels is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can ask at least 4 classroom questions.
teacherNotes: Require rising intonation in oral practice.

short-academic-texts.title: Schedule, Lesson, Homework
short-academic-texts.objective: Discuss class schedule, lesson timing, and homework expectations.
short-academic-texts.content: Short academic texts is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can state class schedule and homework due time.
teacherNotes: Tie examples to real student timetable.

lecture-listening-cues.title: Asking for Help and Clarification
lecture-listening-cues.objective: Ask for repetition, slower speech, and explanation politely.
lecture-listening-cues.content: Lecture listening cues is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can request repetition or explanation politely.

note-taking-phrases.title: Scientific Russian I: What is…?
note-taking-phrases.objective: Define simple scientific concepts using beginner Russian patterns.
note-taking-phrases.content: Note-taking phrases is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can answer “Что такое …?” with a short definition.

short-written-responses.title: Scientific Russian II: Classification
short-written-responses.objective: Classify simple scientific terms into basic groups.
short-written-responses.content: Short written responses is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can classify simple scientific terms into categories.
teacherNotes: Use sorting cards before writing explanations.

noun-gender-number.title: Scientific Russian III: Process and Cause
noun-gender-number.objective: Describe simple cause-and-process relationships in science contexts.
noun-gender-number.content: Noun gender and number is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can describe a simple process and a cause.

case-pattern-awareness.title: Biology Russian I: Cell and Organism
case-pattern-awareness.objective: Describe cell and organism basics with beginner biology Russian.
case-pattern-awareness.content: Case pattern awareness is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can describe a cell as part of an organism.
Can name basic organism-related terms.
teacherNotes: Focus on concept accuracy before grammar perfection.

adjective-agreement-basics.title: Adjective Agreement Basics
adjective-agreement-basics.objective: Use basic adjective-noun agreement in beginner academic phrases.
adjective-agreement-basics.content: Adjective agreement basics is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can form simple adjective+noun phrases in singular/plural.
teacherNotes: Prioritize phrase chunks over full paradigm memorization.

present-past-future.title: Present, Past, Future
present-past-future.objective: Use simple time references to describe study actions.
present-past-future.content: Present, past, and future is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can produce one sentence each for present, past, future.
teacherNotes: Use timeline visuals for tense anchoring.

motion-verbs-intro.title: Motion Verbs Intro
motion-verbs-intro.objective: Use beginner motion verbs for classroom and campus movement.
motion-verbs-intro.content: Motion verbs intro is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can describe movement to/from class with basic verbs.
teacherNotes: Drill direction pairs with gestures and maps.

schedules-deadlines.title: Schedules and Deadlines
schedules-deadlines.objective: Communicate deadlines and schedule updates clearly.
schedules-deadlines.content: Schedules and deadlines is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can state deadlines and ask for timing clarification.

checkpoint-01-a.title: Checkpoint 01 A
checkpoint-01-a.objective: Mixed checkpoint on literacy and classroom foundations (guided mini-assessment).
checkpoint-01-a.content: Checkpoint 01 A is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can complete mixed recognition and production checkpoint tasks.
teacherNotes: Use as low-stakes diagnostic; note patterns for remediation.

checkpoint-01-b.title: Checkpoint 01 B
checkpoint-01-b.objective: Second checkpoint pass with stronger task production and recycle pressure.
checkpoint-01-b.content: Checkpoint 01 B is part of the first runnable slice of the Russian shared-core path. Complete this lesson to advance your lesson progression, readiness, and checkpoint eligibility.
canDoOutcomes: Can complete mixed checkpoint production tasks with minimal prompts.

cefr.explainedIntro: CEFR (Common European Framework of Reference) is the international standard for measuring language proficiency. Here's what each level means:
cefr.A0_desc: No prior knowledge. You start from scratch learning the alphabet and basic sounds.
cefr.A1_desc: You can understand and use basic everyday expressions and introduce yourself.
cefr.A2_desc: You can communicate in simple, routine tasks and describe your background.
cefr.B1_desc: You can handle most travel situations and describe experiences, plans and opinions.
cefr.B2_desc: You can interact fluently with native speakers and produce detailed text on various topics.

alphabet.title: The Russian Alphabet
alphabet.objective: Learn the 33 letters of the Russian alphabet and their sounds.
alphabet.content:
The Russian alphabet (Русский алфавит) has 33 letters: 10 vowels, 21 consonants, and 2 signs.

Vowels: А, Е, Ё, И, О, У, Ы, Э, Ю, Я

Some letters look like English letters but sound different:
• В = V (not B)
• Н = N (not H)
• Р = R (not P)
• С = S (not C)
• У = U (not Y)

Practice writing and saying each letter aloud. Focus on the ones that look familiar but sound different.

pronunciation.title: Pronunciation Basics
pronunciation.objective: Understand Russian pronunciation rules including stress and vowel reduction.
pronunciation.content:
Russian pronunciation has key rules:

1. Stress matters: The stressed vowel in a word is pronounced clearly. Unstressed vowels change sound.

2. Vowel reduction: Unstressed О sounds like А. Unstressed Е sounds closer to И.

3. Voiced/voiceless: At the end of a word, voiced consonants become voiceless:
• Б → П, В → Ф, Г → К, Д → Т

4. Soft and hard consonants: Most consonants have soft and hard versions. A soft sign Ь makes the preceding consonant soft.

greetings.title: Greetings & Polite Phrases
greetings.objective: Learn essential Russian greetings and polite expressions for daily interaction.
greetings.content:
Basic greetings:

• Здравствуйте (Zdravstvuyte) — Hello (formal)
• Привет (Privet) — Hi (informal)
• Доброе утро (Dobroye utro) — Good morning
• Добрый день (Dobryy den') — Good afternoon
• Добрый вечер (Dobryy vecher) — Good evening

Polite phrases:
• Спасибо (Spasibo) — Thank you
• Пожалуйста (Pozhaluysta) — Please / You're welcome
• Извините (Izvinite) — Excuse me
• До свидания (Do svidaniya) — Goodbye

introducing.title: Introducing Yourself
introducing.objective: Learn to introduce yourself, say your name, and ask basic questions.
introducing.content:
Key phrases:

• Меня зовут... (Menya zovut...) — My name is...
• Как вас зовут? (Kak vas zovut?) — What is your name? (formal)
• Как тебя зовут? (Kak tebya zovut?) — What is your name? (informal)
• Я студент/студентка (Ya student/studentka) — I am a student (m/f)
• Я из... (Ya iz...) — I am from...
• Очень приятно (Ochen' priyatno) — Nice to meet you

Practice: Create a short self-introduction using these phrases.

vocab.2: Как вас зовут? — What is your name?
vocab.3: Я студент — I am a student
vocab.4: Очень приятно — Nice to meet you

dailyExpressions.title: Daily Expressions
dailyExpressions.objective: Learn common phrases used in everyday Russian life.
dailyExpressions.content:
Useful daily phrases:

• Да (Da) — Yes
• Нет (Net) — No
• Я не понимаю (Ya ne ponimayu) — I don't understand
• Вы говорите по-английски? (Vy govorite po-angliyski?) — Do you speak English?
• Сколько стоит? (Skol'ko stoit?) — How much does it cost?
• Где находится...? (Gde nakhoditsya...?) — Where is...?
• Можно...? (Mozhno...?) — May I...? / Is it possible...?

These phrases will help you navigate daily situations in Russia.

vocab.2: Я не понимаю — I don't understand

numbersDates.title: Numbers & Dates
numbersDates.objective: Learn to count in Russian and understand dates.
numbersDates.content:
Numbers 1-10:
1 — Один (Odin)
2 — Два (Dva)
3 — Три (Tri)
4 — Четыре (Chetyre)
5 — Пять (Pyat')
6 — Шесть (Shest')
7 — Семь (Sem')
8 — Восемь (Vosem')
9 — Девять (Devyat')
10 — Десять (Desyat')

Days of the week:
Понедельник — Monday
Вторник — Tuesday
Среда — Wednesday
Четверг — Thursday
Пятница — Friday
Суббота — Saturday
Воскресенье — Sunday

vocab.1: Один, два, три — One, two, three

askingDirections.title: Asking for Directions
askingDirections.objective: Learn to ask for and understand basic directions in Russian.
askingDirections.content:
Direction phrases:

• Где...? (Gde...?) — Where is...?
• Как пройти до...? (Kak proyti do...?) — How do I get to...?
• Налево (Nalevo) — To the left
• Направо (Napravo) — To the right
• Прямо (Pryamo) — Straight ahead
• Далеко (Daleko) — Far
• Близко (Blizko) — Close/Near
• Остановка (Ostanovka) — Bus stop
• Метро (Metro) — Metro/Subway
• Университет (Universitet) — University

universityVocab.title: University Vocabulary
universityVocab.objective: Learn essential vocabulary for university life in Russia.
universityVocab.content:
University terms:

• Университет (Universitet) — University
• Факультет (Fakul'tet) — Faculty/Department
• Лекция (Lektsiya) — Lecture
• Семинар (Seminar) — Seminar
• Экзамен (Ekzamen) — Exam
• Зачёт (Zachyot) — Credit/Pass
• Расписание (Raspisaniye) — Schedule/Timetable
• Общежитие (Obshchezhitiye) — Dormitory
• Библиотека (Biblioteka) — Library
• Столовая (Stolovaya) — Cafeteria

classroomPhrases.title: Classroom Phrases
classroomPhrases.objective: Learn phrases you'll use in lectures and seminars.
classroomPhrases.content:
In the classroom:

• Можно вопрос? (Mozhno vopros?) — May I ask a question?
• Повторите, пожалуйста (Povtorite, pozhaluysta) — Please repeat
• Я не понял/поняла (Ya ne ponyal/ponyala) — I didn't understand (m/f)
• Можно выйти? (Mozhno vyyti?) — May I leave?
• Какое домашнее задание? (Kakoye domashneye zadaniye?) — What's the homework?
• Когда экзамен? (Kogda ekzamen?) — When is the exam?
• Спасибо за лекцию (Spasibo za lektsiyu) — Thank you for the lecture

vocab.1: Можно вопрос? — May I ask a question?
vocab.3: Когда экзамен? — When is the exam?

readingBasics.title: Reading Basics
readingBasics.objective: Start reading simple Russian texts and signs.
readingBasics.content:
Reading practice:

Common signs you'll see:
• Вход (Vkhod) — Entrance
• Выход (Vykhod) — Exit
• Открыто (Otkryto) — Open
• Закрыто (Zakryto) — Closed
• Касса (Kassa) — Cashier
• Туалет (Tualet) — Toilet
• Аптека (Apteka) — Pharmacy
• Магазин (Magazin) — Shop

Practice reading these words aloud. Notice how the letters you learned combine into real words.

comprehensiveReview.title: Comprehensive Review
comprehensiveReview.objective: Review all key concepts from the previous modules.
comprehensiveReview.content:
Review checklist:

✅ Can you write all 33 Russian letters?
✅ Do you know the pronunciation rules (stress, vowel reduction)?
✅ Can you greet someone formally and informally?
✅ Can you introduce yourself?
✅ Do you know numbers 1-10?
✅ Can you ask for directions?
✅ Do you know basic university vocabulary?
✅ Can you read simple Russian signs?

Go back and review any section where you feel less confident.

conversationPractice.title: Conversation Practice
conversationPractice.content:
Practice these conversation scenarios:

1. Meeting someone new:
— Здравствуйте! Меня зовут [your name]. Я студент. Я из [your country].
— Очень приятно! Как ваши дела?
— Хорошо, спасибо!

2. At the university:
— Извините, где библиотека?
— Идите прямо, потом направо.
— Спасибо большое!

3. In a shop:
— Сколько стоит это?
— Двести рублей.
— Хорошо, я возьму.

Practice each scenario out loud.

steps.subtitle: Start learning in three simple steps

steps.step3Desc: Pass exams, enter university preparatory programs, or communicate confidently in daily life.

testimonial.quote: The blended approach of AI practice and real teacher sessions helped me pass my university prep exam in just 4 months.
testimonial.author: Ahmed K.

t0.r1: Best Russian teacher! Mohamed Amin truly understands the challenges Arab students face learning Russian. He helped me so much preparing for the preparatory year.
t0.r2: His lessons are well-organized and very useful. He explains grammar in a simple way and connects it to daily conversation. Highly recommended!
t0.r3: A professional and patient teacher. He speaks Arabic which makes understanding difficult concepts much easier in the beginning.

teachers.reassurance: Try with confidence — if the first teacher isn't the right fit, you can try another one for free!

desc.t0: Certified Russian language instructor with extensive experience in preparing Arab students for university life in Russia. Specializes in academic Russian, preparatory year programs, and conversational fluency. Fluent in Arabic, Russian, and English.
desc.t1: Experienced Russian teacher specializing in foreign language education. I offer Russian lessons for all levels — from beginner to advanced, tailored to your knowledge, pace, and goals.
desc.t2: Native Russian speaker — natural and fluent. I live in Baku and offer fun, results-oriented lessons. I am a native English teacher with a specialization in Russian.
desc.t3: From beginner to advanced — discover how to learn Russian! I hold a Master degree in Russian language and literature. During lessons, we learn grammar in a simple way through diverse and fun conversation topics.
desc.t4: Practice Russian conversation with a native speaker! I am from Moscow and currently live in Baku, Azerbaijan. I love outdoor activities and making learning fun.
desc.t5: Native Russian teacher — your guide to the language! I am Akylay, from Kyrgyzstan. I have been teaching for years. Russian and English are my passions.
desc.t6: Enthusiastic experienced teacher | Structured Russian lessons for beginners and exam preparation. I am Oxana, a native Russian speaker living in Europe.

about.t0: I am Mohamed Amin, a certified Russian language instructor from Uzbekistan. Fluent in Arabic, Russian, and English. I have years of experience preparing Arab students for Russian universities, offering personalized lessons tailored to each student's level and goals.
about.t1: Hello! I'm Vasilisa, a Russian teacher from Moscow. I hold a degree in teaching Russian as a foreign language. I offer fun and structured lessons covering grammar, conversation, and Russian culture.
about.t2: I'm Gulnara, a native Russian speaker specializing in teaching Arab students. Based in Baku, I offer flexible lessons focused on real results and conversational fluency.
about.t3: I'm Dmitry, holding a Master's in Russian literature. I teach from beginners to advanced with focus on grammar and correct pronunciation through diverse conversation topics.
about.t4: Hi! I'm Alina from Moscow. I love teaching Russian in a fun and practical way, focusing on daily conversation and useful phrases for life in Russia.
about.t5: My name is Akylay from Kyrgyzstan. I've been teaching Russian for years at all levels. I specialize in TORFL preparation and academic writing.
about.t6: I'm Oxana, fluent in Russian and German. I offer structured lessons for beginners and exam preparation. Based in Europe, I love helping students on their Russian journey.

student.headline: Your academic decision deserves sharper intelligence
student.subheadline: ORYXA is a decision intelligence layer — helping you understand your real options, compare programs deeply, and move from confusion to a clear choice.
student.f1: Deeper understanding of what fits your path
student.f3: From uncertainty to a clear next step
student.trust: Built to turn random searching into informed decision-making.

institution.headline: Professional institutional presence in front of the right students
institution.subheadline: A complete institutional portal: claim your institution, manage your programs and page, and track student interest from one place.
institution.trust: Managed, verified institutional access — not a public account.

hero.headline: ORYXA doesn't search for you — it understands what fits you
hero.subheadline: Instead of thousands of random options, ORYXA reads your academic, financial, and career reality, then narrows choices to what actually deserves your time.

flow.headline: How ORYXA Works
flow.subheadline: A multi-layered decision path — not just filter-based search.

country.label: Layer 1
country.title: Country Environment Evaluation
country.desc: Safety, cost of living, post-graduation opportunities, quality of life.

university.label: Layer 2
university.title: University Strength Analysis
university.desc: Not just reputation — curriculum freshness, accreditation, infrastructure, and international student support.

program.label: Layer 3
program.title: Program Fit Assessment
program.desc: Is the program future-ready? Does it connect to the job market? Does it match your path?

student.label: Layer 4
student.title: Reading the Student's Reality
student.desc: Your budget, GPA, career direction, ability to work during study — everything factors in.

match.label: Result
match.title: Intelligent Narrowing
match.desc: Instead of 500 random options, you get the ones that genuinely fit your reality.

dimensions.headline: What Does ORYXA See in You?
dimensions.subheadline: ORYXA doesn't rely on one or two filters. It reads 56 different dimensions to build a real picture of what suits you.

different.headline: Why ORYXA Is Different
different.subheadline: The difference between random search and intelligent decision.

search.title: Not a Search Engine
search.desc: Search engines give you everything. ORYXA gives you only what fits — based on your reality, not alphabetical order.

reputation.title: Not a Reputation Ranking
reputation.desc: Traditional rankings measure fame. ORX Rank measures future-readiness and program fit for your path.

noise.title: Less Noise, Clearer Decision
noise.desc: Instead of getting lost among hundreds of similar options, ORYXA reduces noise and highlights what truly deserves your consideration.

aiEra.headline: In the Age of AI — Academic Decisions Need Intelligence Too
aiEra.body: The world is changing fast. Fields that were in demand 5 years ago may not be in 5 years. ORYXA helps you make a decision built on the future — not the past.
